nativeRange.habitat: Highland lake and tributary streams at approximately 793 m elevation; clear, well-oxygenated water with dense aquatic vegetation and steep mountain surroundings (1200–1500 m)

decorPreferences: dense plantings (Java moss, water sprite, hornwort, Vallisneria)
floating plants to diffuse light and deter jumping
fine-leaved plants or spawning mops for egg deposition
subdued or moderate lighting to enhance yellow coloration

diet.notes: Accepts high-quality flake, micro-pellets, and spirulina-based foods as a staple. Live and frozen foods (brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, mosquito larvae) should be offered regularly to maintain vibrant coloration; a diet limited to dry foods alone tends to dull color. Feed small portions two to three times daily.

avoidWith: very small nano fish that may be outcompeted at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Melanotaenia herbertaxelrodi; confirm availability before use.

summary: A striking single-lake endemic from Lake Tebera, Papua New Guinea, males display brilliant yellow bodies with a blue-to-black mid-lateral stripe and red-tinged fins. IUCN Vulnerable due to its extremely restricted range. Best kept in schools of 6–8 in well-planted, moderately hard, alkaline water.
careNotes: The Lake Tebera Rainbowfish is endemic to Lake Tebera and its immediate tributary streams in the Southern Highlands of Papua New Guinea at around 793 m elevation — one of the most restricted natural ranges of any traded rainbowfish, which has resulted in an IUCN Vulnerable listing (assessed December 2019). The aquarium trade relies almost entirely on captive-bred stock, which is easily produced, making wild collection unnecessary and inadvisable. Males reach around 10–12 cm and develop deep bodies with vivid yellow coloration and a contrasting blue-to-black lateral stripe; females are smaller and less intensely colored. A tank of at least 150 L with a minimum 100 cm footprint is recommended to accommodate a shoal of 6–8 fish and allow the open-water swimming this active species requires. Moderate hardness (gH 8–18) and alkalinity (pH 7.5–8.0) matching the fish’s highland lake origin should be maintained; soft or acidic water is not suitable. Water quality must be kept high — regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly) and efficient filtration are essential. This species is an active, skittish jumper; a tight-fitting lid is required. Floating plants help reduce jumping and provide a sense of security. Coloration is most intense in well-lit planted tanks with dark substrate, and during morning hours when males actively display to one another.
breedingNotes: Lake Tebera Rainbowfish are egg scatterers that spawn readily in the aquarium when provided with suitable conditions. A dedicated breeding tank of at least 80 L with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, Cabomba) or spawning mops is recommended; slightly hard, alkaline water (pH ∼7.5, gH 10–15, temperature 22–25°C) triggers spawning activity. Males display an intensified white-to-blue stripe on the head when courting. Eggs are adhesive and deposited singly among vegetation over several days; parents will consume eggs and must be removed or eggs transferred to a separate raising tank. Eggs hatch in 7–12 days depending on temperature. Fry are tiny at first and require infusoria or commercial fry foods for the first week, transitioning to finely crushed flake and newly hatched brine shrimp as they grow.

primary.name: FishBase — Melanotaenia herbertaxelrodi
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and parameter source. Provided adult size (max 15.0 cm TL), temperature range (20–26°C), pH (7.5–7.8), hardness (10+ dH), IUCN Vulnerable status (assessed December 6, 2019), native range (Lake Tebera basin, Southern Highlands, Papua New Guinea, 793 m elevation), taxonomy (Melanotaeniidae, Atheriniformes), and trophic level 2.9 (omnivore).

additional.name: AquaticCommunity — Lake Tebera Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (≥12 cm), temperature (21–25°C), alkaline pH (7.6–7.8), dense planting recommendation, omnivorous diet notes, and that poor diet causes dull coloration.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Melanotaenia herbertaxelrodi
additional.notes: Provided adult size range (8–10 cm), temperature (21–24°C), pH (7–8), hardness (8–18°N), minimum tank 80 L, lifespan (3–5 years), peaceful temperament, mid-water swim zone, group size of 6+ recommended, breeding notes (eggs hatch 7–10 days, fry require infusoria).

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Axelrodi Rainbow
additional.notes: Provided hardness range (10–15 dGH), pH (7.5–8.0), temperature (68–79°F / 20–26°C), tank size (55 gal / ~208 L for larger groups), lifespan (6–8 years), jump-risk note, schooling group size (6–8), and breeding details (eggs hatch 7–13 days).

additional.name: Quality Marine — A Rainbow of Yellow
additional.notes: Confirmed endemic status to Lake Tebera basin (2600+ ft elevation), vulnerability due to constricted range, ease of aquaculture reducing need for wild collection, clear water habitat with dense vegetation, morning activity and coloration peak, and minimum group/tank guidance.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; spawns readily in well-planted tanks or with spawning mops. Separate eggs or adults to prevent predation. Eggs hatch in 7–12 days; fry need infusoria initially.
